era_context: The early 1880s fell within the high tide of European imperialism and the second industrial revolution. The great powers were expanding colonial claims across Africa, Asia, and the Pacific while rapid industrialisation transformed economies and societies. In Europe, Bismarck's alliance system maintained a fragile great-power peace, but nationalist and revolutionary movements — from Russian populism to Irish Home Rule — challenged established orders. In the Americas, the United States was consolidating its continental reach after Reconstruction, while South American nations fought border wars. In Asia, the Qing dynasty faced internal and external pressures, Meiji Japan was modernising at speed, and the Ottoman Empire continued its long territorial contraction.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Sub-Saharan Africa beyond colonial and missionary records
Indigenous Australian communities and Pacific Island societies
Central Asian communities under Russian expansion
Rural Southeast Asian societies outside colonial capitals
Pre-literate societies across Oceania and the Arctic

events.title: Assassination of Tsar Alexander II of Russia
events.description: On 13 March 1881 (1 March Old Style), Tsar Alexander II was killed by a bomb thrown by Ignacy Hryniewiecki, a member of the revolutionary organisation Narodnaya Volya (People's Will). A first bomb, thrown by Nikolai Rysakov, damaged the Tsar's carriage but did not injure him; when Alexander stepped out to inspect the damage and tend to wounded bystanders, Hryniewiecki threw a second bomb at his feet, mortally wounding both of them. Alexander II, known as the 'Tsar Liberator' for emancipating the serfs in 1861, had been on the verge of approving limited constitutional reforms. His son Alexander III immediately reversed course, embarking on a programme of autocratic reaction, Russification, and repression that would define Russian politics for a generation and contribute to the revolutionary pressures that ultimately destroyed the Romanov dynasty.
key_figures: Ignacy Hryniewiecki (Narodnaya Volya operative, killed in attack)
Sophia Perovskaya (Narodnaya Volya organiser of the plot)
Konstantin Pobedonostsev (advisor to Alexander III, architect of reaction)

sources.name: Contemporary police reports and trial transcripts of the assassination conspirators (1881)
Edvard Radzinsky, Alexander II: The Last Great Tsar (2005)
Contemporary press coverage across European newspapers, March 1881

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented by police records, court proceedings, diplomatic cables, and press coverage. No substantive dispute about facts.

events.title: Anti-Jewish pogroms erupt across the Russian Empire
events.region: Russian Empire (Ukraine, southern Russia, Congress Poland)
events.description: Beginning in April 1881, a wave of anti-Jewish violence swept through southern and southwestern provinces of the Russian Empire, starting in Yelisavetgrad (modern Kropyvnytskyi, Ukraine) and spreading to Kiev, Odessa, Warsaw, and dozens of smaller towns. The pogroms — involving looting, arson, assault, and murder — were triggered in part by false rumours blaming Jews for the assassination of Alexander II. Local authorities often responded slowly or with complicity. The government of Alexander III subsequently enacted the May Laws of 1882, restricting Jewish residence and economic activity. The pogroms inaugurated a decades-long cycle of violence that drove mass Jewish emigration to the Americas and Palestine, fundamentally reshaping global Jewish demography and fuelling the early Zionist movement.
key_figures: Nikolai Ignatiev (Minister of the Interior, implemented restrictive policies)
Konstantin Pobedonostsev (Procurator of the Holy Synod, ideological influence)

sources.name: Contemporary reports from the Jewish press and communal records, 1881–1882
John Klier, Russians, Jews, and the Pogroms of 1881–1882 (2011)
Official Russian government investigation reports, 1881–1882

events.certainty_note: Well-documented by both official and communal sources. The scale and extent of government complicity remain subjects of scholarly debate.

events.title: Battle of Majuba Hill — British defeat in First Boer War
events.description: On 27 February 1881, Boer forces under Commandant-General Nicolaas Smit stormed the summit of Majuba Hill, inflicting a decisive defeat on British troops commanded by Major-General Sir George Pomeroy Colley, who was killed in the action. The British suffered 92 killed, 134 wounded, and 59 captured out of a force of approximately 400, while Boer casualties were minimal. The battle was the culminating engagement of the First Boer War (1880–1881) and forced the British government under Gladstone to negotiate. It became a potent symbol of Boer martial pride and British imperial humiliation, with the rallying cry 'Remember Majuba' resurfacing during the Second Boer War two decades later.
key_figures: Sir George Pomeroy Colley (British Major-General, killed)

sources.name: British War Office despatches, February–March 1881
John Laband, The Transvaal Rebellion: The First Boer War, 1880–1881 (2005)
Boer participant accounts collected by Gustav Preller

events.certainty_note: Well-documented by both British military records and Boer accounts. No dating or factual disputes.

events.title: Pretoria Convention ends the First Boer War
events.description: Signed on 3 August 1881, the Pretoria Convention ended the First Boer War by granting the South African Republic (Transvaal) self-governance under nominal British suzerainty. Britain retained control over foreign affairs and the rights of native Africans (a clause largely unenforced in practice). The convention was widely seen in Britain as a humiliating retreat, driven by Gladstone's Liberal government's reluctance to pursue a costly colonial war. The ambiguous sovereignty arrangement — particularly the ill-defined 'suzerainty' clause — became a source of persistent Anglo-Boer friction and was revised by the London Convention of 1884, setting the stage for the far larger Second Boer War in 1899.
key_figures: Paul Kruger (Boer leader, later President of the South African Republic)

sources.name: Text of the Pretoria Convention, 3 August 1881
Thomas Pakenham, The Boer War (1979)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text is extant and undisputed.

events.title: Treaty of Bardo — France establishes protectorate over Tunisia
events.description: On 12 May 1881, Muhammad III as-Sadiq, Bey of Tunis, signed the Treaty of Bardo (Ksar Said) under French military pressure, establishing a French protectorate over Tunisia. France had invaded with approximately 36,000 troops in April, using border raids by Khroumir tribesmen as a pretext. The real motives were strategic: preventing Italian expansion and securing France's position vis-à-vis Algeria. The treaty provoked outrage in Italy, which had its own designs on Tunisia and a significant settler population there, driving Italy towards the Triple Alliance with Germany and Austria-Hungary. Tunisia would remain under French control until independence in 1956.

sources.name: Text of the Treaty of Bardo, 12 May 1881
French parliamentary debates on the Tunisian expedition, April–May 1881
Julia Clancy-Smith, Rebel and Saint: Muslim Notables, Populist Protest, Colonial Encounters (1994)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text is extant; events are well-documented by French, Tunisian, and Italian sources.

events.title: Assassination of US President James A. Garfield
events.region: United States (Washington, D.C. / Elberon, New Jersey)
events.description: On 2 July 1881, President James A. Garfield was shot at the Baltimore and Potomac Railroad Station in Washington, D.C. by Charles J. Guiteau, a disappointed office-seeker who believed he was owed a diplomatic appointment. Garfield lingered for 79 days, with his doctors' probing of the wound with unsterilised instruments likely worsening his condition through sepsis. He died on 19 September 1881. The assassination galvanised the civil service reform movement, leading directly to the Pendleton Civil Service Reform Act of 1883, which replaced the spoils system with merit-based federal appointments — one of the most significant administrative reforms in American government history.
key_figures: James A. Garfield (20th President of the United States, assassinated)
Chester A. Arthur (Vice President, succeeded Garfield)

sources.name: Trial transcripts of United States v. Guiteau (1881–1882)
Candice Millard, Destiny of the Republic: A Tale of Madness, Medicine, and the Murder of a President (2011)
Contemporary press coverage (New York Times, Washington Post, etc.), July–September 1881

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented. No factual dispute, though debate continues about whether Garfield might have survived with modern medical care.

events.title: Muhammad Ahmad proclaims himself the Mahdi in Sudan
events.description: In June 1881, Muhammad Ahmad ibn Abd Allah, a Sufi religious leader from Dongola, publicly declared himself the Mahdi — the divinely guided redeemer of Islam — on Aba Island in the White Nile. His proclamation attracted followers from across Sudan, drawing on widespread resentment against the Turco-Egyptian administration, its heavy taxation, and its campaign against the slave trade. After repulsing an initial Egyptian military expedition in August, the Mahdist movement rapidly grew into a full-scale revolt. Over the next four years, the Mahdists would conquer most of Sudan, culminating in the fall of Khartoum and the death of General Gordon in 1885, establishing an independent Mahdist state that lasted until 1898.
key_figures: Muhammad Ahmad ibn Abd Allah (the Mahdi)

sources.name: Muhammad Ahmad's own proclamation letters (extant in Sudanese archives)
P.M. Holt, The Mahdist State in the Sudan 1881–1898 (1958; 2nd ed. 1970)
Egyptian government despatches from Khartoum, 1881

events.certainty_note: Date of proclamation is established by contemporary correspondence. Some sources place it slightly earlier or later within the summer of 1881.

events.title: Chilean forces capture Lima in the War of the Pacific
events.description: On 17 January 1881, Chilean forces entered and occupied Lima, the Peruvian capital, following decisive victories at the battles of Chorrillos and Miraflores (13 and 15 January). The battles were among the bloodiest of the War of the Pacific (1879–1884), with combined casualties in the thousands on both sides. The occupation of Lima effectively decided the war in Chile's favour, though guerrilla resistance under Andrés Avelino Cáceres continued in the Peruvian highlands for years. The war resulted in Peru losing the province of Tarapacá and Bolivia losing its entire coastal territory, reshaping South American geopolitics permanently.
key_figures: Patricio Lynch (Chilean commander of the occupation)

sources.name: Chilean War Ministry records and military despatches, January 1881
William F. Sater, Andean Tragedy: Fighting the War of the Pacific, 1879–1884 (2007)
Contemporary foreign diplomatic correspondence (US, British legations in Lima)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by military records, diplomatic sources, and press coverage from multiple nations.

events.title: Urabi revolt begins in Egypt
events.description: In September 1881, Colonel Ahmed Urabi led a military demonstration at Abdin Palace in Cairo, demanding the dismissal of the war minister, an increase in the size of the army, and the formation of a representative assembly. Urabi's movement, rooted in the Egyptian officer corps' resentment of Turco-Circassian elite dominance and of Anglo-French financial control through the Dual Control system, rapidly gained popular support across Egypt. Khedive Tewfik was forced to accede to the demands and appoint a new government sympathetic to the movement. The Urabi revolt is regarded as one of the earliest expressions of Egyptian nationalism, though it would be crushed by British military intervention at the Battle of Tel el-Kebir in September 1882, leading to British occupation of Egypt.
key_figures: Ahmed Urabi (Colonel, leader of the nationalist movement)
Mahmoud Sami el-Baroudi (Prime Minister sympathetic to Urabi)

sources.name: Ahmed Urabi's memoirs (written in exile, published posthumously)
British and French consular despatches from Cairo, September 1881
Juan Cole, Colonialism and Revolution in the Middle East: Social and Cultural Origins of Egypt's 'Urabi Movement (1993)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented by European consular records and Egyptian sources. Exact dating of the Abdin Palace demonstration (9 September 1881) is confirmed.

events.title: Pasteur demonstrates anthrax vaccine at Pouilly-le-Fort
events.description: In May–June 1881, Louis Pasteur conducted a dramatic public experiment at a farm near Pouilly-le-Fort to demonstrate the efficacy of his anthrax vaccine. Twenty-five sheep were vaccinated with two doses of attenuated anthrax bacilli, then challenged along with twenty-five unvaccinated control sheep with virulent anthrax. On 2 June, all vaccinated sheep were alive and healthy; all unvaccinated sheep were dead or dying. The experiment was a sensation, reported across Europe, and established the principle of vaccination with attenuated pathogens as a practical preventive measure. It built directly on Edward Jenner's earlier smallpox work and laid the foundations for modern immunology and veterinary medicine.
key_figures: Hippolyte Rossignol (veterinarian who organised the trial)

sources.name: Pasteur's reports to the Académie des Sciences, 1881
Gerald Geison, The Private Science of Louis Pasteur (1995)
Contemporary press accounts in Le Temps and other French newspapers, June 1881

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented. However, Gerald Geison's 1995 analysis of Pasteur's laboratory notebooks revealed that Pasteur used a different vaccine preparation (chemical attenuation by Chamberland's method) than he publicly claimed (oxygen-attenuated), raising questions about his scientific ethics while not disputing the result.

events.title: Founding of the Jiyūtō (Liberal Party) in Japan
events.description: On 29 October 1881, Itagaki Taisuke formally established the Jiyūtō (Liberal Party), Japan's first modern political party. The party emerged from the Freedom and People's Rights Movement (Jiyū Minken Undō) and advocated for popular sovereignty, a national assembly, and a constitution based on French liberal principles. Its founding was closely tied to the Political Crisis of 1881 (Meiji 14 no Seihen), in which the government ousted Ōkuma Shigenobu and simultaneously issued an Imperial Rescript promising a constitution and national assembly by 1890. The Jiyūtō drew its base from rural landowners and former samurai, and despite being dissolved in 1884 under government pressure, it established the template for party politics in Meiji Japan.
key_figures: Itagaki Taisuke (founder, former samurai and councillor)
Ōkuma Shigenobu (ousted from government in the 1881 crisis)
Itō Hirobumi (government leader who orchestrated the 1881 crisis)

sources.name: Founding charter and platform of the Jiyūtō, October 1881
Andrew Gordon, A Modern History of Japan: From Tokugawa Times to the Present (2003; 4th ed. 2020)
Meiji government records and newspaper coverage (Tokyo Nichi Nichi Shimbun, Yūbin Hōchi Shimbun)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Japanese political and press records.

events.title: Sitting Bull surrenders at Fort Buford
events.description: On 19 July 1881, Hunkpapa Lakota leader Sitting Bull surrendered to US Army forces at Fort Buford in Dakota Territory, ending his four-year exile in Canada. After the victory at the Little Bighorn in 1876, Sitting Bull had led his band across the border into the Northwest Territories, but diminishing buffalo herds and Canadian government reluctance to provide rations made the position untenable. His surrender effectively marked the end of major Lakota armed resistance to US expansion on the northern Great Plains. Sitting Bull was held as a prisoner of war at Fort Randall for nearly two years before being transferred to the Standing Rock Reservation.
key_figures: Sitting Bull (Tȟatȟáŋka Íyotake, Hunkpapa Lakota leader)
Major David Brotherton (Fort Buford commander who received the surrender)

sources.name: US Army records, Fort Buford, July 1881
Robert Utley, The Lance and the Shield: The Life and Times of Sitting Bull (1993)
Contemporary press coverage (Bismarck Tribune, New York Herald)

events.certainty_note: Date and circumstances well-established by US military records and press coverage.

events.title: American Red Cross founded by Clara Barton
events.description: On 21 May 1881, Clara Barton established the American Association of the Red Cross (later the American Red Cross) in Washington, D.C. Barton, who had served as a nurse during the American Civil War and had observed the International Red Cross during the Franco-Prussian War, had campaigned for years to bring the United States into the international Red Cross movement. The US had resisted joining the Geneva Convention of 1864, partly due to isolationist sentiment, but Barton's organisation eventually succeeded in securing American ratification in 1882. The American Red Cross would grow into one of the country's most significant humanitarian institutions.

sources.name: Founding documents of the American Association of the Red Cross, May 1881
Elizabeth Brown Pryor, Clara Barton: Professional Angel (1987)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented by institutional records and Barton's own extensive correspondence.

events.title: Thessaly and part of Epirus ceded to Greece by the Ottoman Empire
events.description: On 2 July 1881, following prolonged negotiations mediated by the great powers at the Constantinople Conference, the Ottoman Empire formally ceded most of Thessaly and the Arta district of Epirus to the Kingdom of Greece. The transfer, agreed upon in principle at the Congress of Berlin in 1878, significantly expanded Greek territory and population without warfare. However, the cession left substantial Greek-speaking populations in Ottoman territory (particularly in Macedonia and remaining Epirus), fuelling irredentist ambitions that would drive Greek foreign policy for decades and contribute to the Balkan Wars of 1912–1913.

sources.name: Convention of Constantinople, 2 July 1881 (treaty text)
Richard Clogg, A Concise History of Greece (1992; 3rd ed. 2013)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and diplomatic records are extant. No factual dispute.

events.title: Natural History Museum opens in London
events.description: On 18 April 1881, the British Museum (Natural History) — now the Natural History Museum — opened its doors in its purpose-built Romanesque Revival building in South Kensington, designed by Alfred Waterhouse. The collections had been separated from the main British Museum in Bloomsbury under the advocacy of Richard Owen, the museum's superintendent, who argued that the natural history collections needed dedicated exhibition and research space. The museum became one of the world's leading institutions for natural science research and public education, housing specimens collected by Darwin, Wallace, and other major naturalists.
key_figures: Richard Owen (superintendent and advocate for the separate museum)

sources.name: Natural History Museum institutional records, 1881
William T. Stearn, The Natural History Museum at South Kensington (1981)

events.certainty_note: Opening date and circumstances well-documented.

events.title: First public electricity supply at Godalming, England
events.description: In autumn 1881, the town of Godalming in Surrey became the first place in the world to have a public electricity supply, with a hydroelectric generator on the River Wey powering arc lamps for street lighting and some private consumers. The scheme, implemented by Calder & Barrett using a Siemens alternator, replaced the existing gas lighting. Although the project was short-lived — it was abandoned by 1884 due to financial and technical difficulties — it demonstrated the viability of public electricity supply and preceded Thomas Edison's Pearl Street Station in New York (1882). The Godalming experiment represents an early milestone in the electrification of public life.

sources.name: Godalming Borough Council records, 1881
Brian Bowers, A History of Electric Light and Power (1982)
The Electrician (journal), contemporary reports 1881–1882

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in municipal and engineering records. Priority claims are sometimes contested vis-à-vis other early electrical installations.

events.title: Gunfight at the O.K. Corral
events.description: On 26 October 1881, a brief but famous gunfight erupted near the O.K. Corral in Tombstone, Arizona Territory, between lawmen Virgil, Wyatt, and Morgan Earp (accompanied by John Henry 'Doc' Holliday) and a group of outlaw Cowboys including Billy Clanton, Tom McLaury, and Frank McLaury. The 30-second fight killed three Cowboys and wounded Virgil and Morgan Earp and Doc Holliday. The event, rooted in local political and economic rivalries between Republican-aligned townspeople and Democrat-aligned rural ranchers, became the most famous gunfight of the American frontier era, though its broader significance is primarily cultural — as the archetypal symbol of Western frontier violence and the contested boundary between law and lawlessness.

sources.name: Tombstone Epitaph and Tombstone Nugget, contemporary newspaper accounts, October–November 1881
Spicer Hearing testimony transcripts, October–November 1881
Jeff Guinn, The Last Gunfight: The Real Story of the Shootout at the O.K. Corral (2011)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented by court records and press. The actual location was in a vacant lot near the corral, not inside it. Many details are contested between Earp and Clanton partisans, but the basic facts are established.

events.title: First International Electrical Exhibition opens in Paris
events.description: From August to November 1881, the International Exposition of Electricity was held at the Palais de l'Industrie in Paris, the first international exhibition devoted entirely to electrical technology. It showcased Edison's incandescent lamp, Alexander Graham Bell's telephone, Siemens' electric railway, and numerous dynamos and electrical devices. The accompanying International Congress of Electricians standardised key electrical units — the volt, ampere, ohm, and farad — which remain in use today. The exhibition powerfully demonstrated that electricity was transitioning from scientific curiosity to practical technology, accelerating investment in electrification across Europe and North America.
key_figures: Adolphe Cochery (French Minister of Posts and Telegraphs, patron)

sources.name: Official catalogue of the Exposition Internationale d'Électricité, Paris, 1881
Proceedings of the International Congress of Electricians, Paris, 1881
Jill Jonnes, Empires of Light: Edison, Tesla, Westinghouse, and the Race to Electrify the World (2003)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by official publications, participant accounts, and press coverage.

events.title: Death of Billy the Kid
events.region: United States (Fort Sumner, New Mexico Territory)
events.description: On 14 July 1881, Sheriff Pat Garrett shot and killed Henry McCarty, known as Billy the Kid, at the Maxwell Ranch in Fort Sumner, New Mexico Territory. Billy the Kid had escaped from the Lincoln County jail on 28 April, killing two deputies, and Garrett had been pursuing him for months. The Kid's brief criminal career — he was approximately 21 at the time of his death — was rooted in the Lincoln County War of 1878, a violent conflict between rival mercantile factions. While his historical significance as a frontier outlaw is modest, his death marked a symbolic end to the most violent phase of the Lincoln County troubles and he rapidly became one of the most mythologised figures of the American West.
key_figures: Henry McCarty / Billy the Kid (outlaw, killed)

sources.name: Pat Garrett, The Authentic Life of Billy, the Kid (1882)
Robert Utley, Billy the Kid: A Short and Violent Life (1989)

events.certainty_note: Date and circumstances confirmed by multiple contemporary sources. Fringe claims that the Kid survived are not supported by credible evidence.

events.title: Alexander III issues reactionary manifesto — era of counter-reforms begins
events.description: On 29 April 1881, Tsar Alexander III issued the Manifesto on Unshakeable Autocracy, drafted by his chief advisor Konstantin Pobedonostsev, decisively rejecting the constitutional reforms his father had been considering before his assassination. The manifesto proclaimed the autocracy's divine mandate and signalled a programme of political repression, censorship, Russification of minority nationalities, and reversal of Alexander II's limited liberalising measures. This set the political tone for the entire reign of Alexander III (1881–1894) and shaped the conditions that would produce both the 1905 and 1917 revolutions.
key_figures: Konstantin Pobedonostsev (Procurator of the Holy Synod, drafter of the manifesto)
Count Loris-Melikov (liberal minister, resigned after the manifesto)

sources.name: Text of the Manifesto on Unshakeable Autocracy, 29 April 1881
Richard Pipes, Russia Under the Old Regime (1974; 2nd ed. 1995)

events.certainty_note: Official document; no factual dispute.

events.title: First Boer War — Battle of Laing's Nek
events.description: On 28 January 1881, British forces under Major-General Colley attempted to force the pass at Laing's Nek on the Natal-Transvaal border and were repulsed by Boer defenders with significant casualties. The British lost 84 killed and 113 wounded out of approximately 480 engaged, while Boer losses were 14 killed and 27 wounded. This was the first major engagement of the First Boer War and demonstrated that traditional British infantry tactics — frontal assaults in close formation — were dangerously obsolete against well-armed irregulars using cover and marksmanship. The lesson would be painfully relearned across the following decades.

sources.name: British War Office despatches, January–February 1881
John Laband, The Transvaal Rebellion: The First Boer War, 1880–1881 (2005)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented by British military records.

events.title: Flogging of Kimberley diamond miners — early South African labour unrest
events.description: Throughout 1881, the Kimberley diamond fields saw increasing regulation of African labour, including the expansion of pass laws and compound systems designed to control the movement and working conditions of Black African mineworkers. The diamond industry's labour practices, including corporal punishment and confinement, were documented by contemporary missionaries and officials. These systems prefigured the formalised racial labour controls that would characterise the later gold mining industry and, eventually, the apartheid state. The year saw growing consolidation of claims by larger capitalists, a process that would culminate in Cecil Rhodes' monopoly through De Beers.
key_figures: Cecil Rhodes (claim holder, rising political figure)
J.B. Robinson (major claim holder and rival)

sources.name: Cape Colony Government Gazette and mining commission reports, 1881
William Worger, South Africa's City of Diamonds: Mine Workers and Monopoly Capitalism in Kimberley, 1867–1895 (1987)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in colonial records, though African workers' own perspectives are poorly represented in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed merit qualification: (1) The role of the Russian government in organising or directing the 1881 pogroms (1881-002) has been significantly revised by modern scholarship. John Klier and others have demonstrated that the pogroms were not centrally orchestrated by the tsarist government, as was long claimed in popular historiography, but rather emerged from local social tensions with varying degrees of official complicity and negligence. (2) Pasteur's Pouilly-le-Fort experiment (1881-010) has been complicated by Gerald Geison's 1995 analysis of Pasteur's laboratory notebooks, which revealed that Pasteur used Chamberland's potassium bichromate chemical attenuation method rather than the oxygen-attenuation method he publicly claimed. While the vaccine worked, Pasteur misrepresented his methodology. (3) The significance of the Godalming electricity scheme (1881-016) as the 'first' public electricity supply is contested — earlier small-scale installations existed, and the distinction between 'public' and 'private' supply is somewhat arbitrary. (4) The Gunfight at the O.K. Corral (1881-017) is commonly described as a law-versus-outlaws encounter, but modern scholarship emphasises it as part of a complex local political and economic factional conflict in which the Earps' motives were not purely civic-minded. (5) The claim that Guiteau was simply a 'disappointed office-seeker' (1881-006) oversimplifies his motivations — he was likely mentally ill and held complex delusional beliefs about his role in Garfield's election.
historiographic_note: 1881 is exhaustively documented across multiple domains: government archives, diplomatic correspondence, press coverage, personal papers, military records, and institutional records survive in abundance from all major powers and many minor ones. The primary biases shaping the record are: (1) European colonial perspectives dominate the sources for African and Middle Eastern events — indigenous voices (Sudanese, Egyptian, South African Black) are filtered through European observers; (2) Russian government sources on the pogroms reflect official self-exculpation; (3) American frontier events are told primarily through the perspectives of white settlers, soldiers, and journalists, with Indigenous perspectives largely absent from contemporary documentation; (4) East Asian events are well-documented in Japanese and Chinese sources but are underrepresented in English-language scholarship relative to European and American events. The year is notable for the extraordinary number of assassinations and attempted assassinations of heads of state, reflecting a global wave of political violence that would continue through the 1890s and early 1900s.

graph_edges.note: Alexander II's assassination directly caused Alexander III's reactionary manifesto
The assassination triggered false rumours blaming Jews, contributing to the pogrom wave
British defeat at Majuba forced negotiation of the Pretoria Convention
French seizure of Tunisia drove Italy into alliance with Germany and Austria-Hungary
Garfield's assassination by a frustrated office-seeker drove civil service reform
The Mahdist revolt led to the siege and fall of Khartoum four years later
The Urabi revolt provoked British military intervention in 1882
Success of the anthrax vaccine led Pasteur to develop the rabies vaccine
Party formation and the 1881 Imperial Rescript promising a constitution led to the Meiji Constitution
Sitting Bull's surrender marked the near-end of armed Lakota resistance; his death in 1890 preceded Wounded Knee
The Paris Electrical Exhibition and Godalming's public supply reflect the same moment of electrical technology becoming practical
Alexander III's reactionary programme created the conditions for revolutionary upheaval
The ambiguous suzerainty terms of the Pretoria Convention became a casus belli for the Second Boer War
Chilean occupation of Lima led to the Treaty of Ancón ending the War of the Pacific
